figures.caption: Fig. 1: Construction of a surface parametrization over the reference simplex ∆2 via closest-point projection from the piecewise affine approximation Γh.
Fig. 4: A high-order triangulated sphere mesh is used to approximate a spherical harmonic. The observed algebraic decay aligns with the fit rate hn−1.

Fig. 2: Interface coupling of two surface elements via continuity of the solution and its binormal derivative. Red points indicate boundary collocation points Ib1 and Ib2 , while blue points represent interface collocation points Is1 and Is2 , aligned for coupling. The right panel highlights the shared interface and point alignment used in the spectral collocation framework.

Fig. 3: A high-order triangular patch mesh is used to discretize the Laplace–Beltrami equation on the upper hemisphere. The spectral discretization exhibits high-order convergence consistent with the rate O(hn−1).
Fig. 7: Turing model solution u1 on the asymmetric torus and Stanford Bunny at times t = 0, 20, and 200 using IMEX-BDF4 with r1 = 0.02, r2 = 0.2.

Fig. 8: Pattern formation process of the reaction–diffusion model on the surface of a sphere corresponding to the concentration of the activator u, simulated at times t = 0,20,200 using the IMEX–BDF4 scheme for different sphere radii.
Fig. 6: Turing model solution u1 on a Swiss cheese surface. Top: r1 = 3.5, r2 = 0 at t = 0,200,600. Bottom: r1 = 0.02, r2 = 0.2 at t = 0,20,200. Simulated with IMEX-BDF4.

Fig. 5: (Left) Computed solution at t = 1 using time step ∆t = 10−3. (Right) L∞-error versus polynomial degree for different mesh sizes h. The simulations were performed using the implicit–explicit backward differentiation formula (IMEX–BDF4) scheme.
